query: site of lesion in endocarditis of rhd

retrieved_docs.title: Aortic Valve Endocarditis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple positive blood cultures, single positive culture for Coxiella burnetii or antiphase I IgG antibody titer greater than 1 to 800 Endocardial involvement (presence of new valvular regurgitation) Presence of vegetation, abscess, or dehiscence (worsening or a change of pre-existing murmur does not meet criteria) Minor clinical criteria are as follows [5] : Predisposing heart condition or use of intravenous drug use Fever with body temperature greater than 38 degrees C (100.4 degrees F) Vascular phenomena: arterial emboli, septic pulmonary infarcts, mycotic aneurysm, intracranial hemorrhage, conjunctival hemorrhages, and Janeway lesion Immunologic phenomena: glomerulonephritis, Osler nodes, Roth spots, rheumatoid factor, and Positive blood culture that does not meet the major criteria

retrieved_docs.title: Neurological Sequelae of Endocarditis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Valvular disease such as rheumatic heart disease and congenital heart abnormalities like a bicuspid aortic valve, ventricular septal defect, coarctation of the aorta are risk factors for infectious endocarditis. Pathogens that account for infectious endocarditis vary and depend on the risk factors including intravenous drug abuse, existing valvular dysfunction, poor oral hygiene, and dental procedure, immunocompromised status, the cardiac implantable electronic device (CIED), or previous history of infectious endocarditis. In infectious endocarditis, neurological complications correlate with the size of vegetation and bacterial load. Among all the causative pathogens, Staphylococci aureus is the most common microorganism of infective endocarditis in the United States and other developed countries. Staphylococci aureus IE is significantly more likely to result in embolic events than other pathogens. Neurological sequelae from IE are as following, Embolic stroke is caused by vegetation debris that lodge in the cerebral blood vessels and ended up resulting in infarction; Transient ischemic attack (TIA) is an episode of neurologic dysfunction attributed to ischemia of brain, spinal cord, or retina; Cerebral hemorrhage Meningitis Brain abscess Toxic encephalopathy Seizure

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Endocarditis -- Evaluation -- Minor Clinical Criteria:
retrieved_docs.content: Predisposition: Intravenous drug use or presence of a predisposing heart condition Fever: Temperature greater than or equal to 38.0 C (100.4 F) Vascular phenomena: Major arterial emboli, septic pulmonary infarcts, mycotic aneurysm, intracranial hemorrhage, conjunctival hemorrhages, or Janeway lesions (non-tender erythematous macules on the palms and soles) Immunologic phenomena: Glomerulonephritis, Osler nodes (tender subcutaneous violaceous nodules mostly on the pads of the fingers and toes, which may also occur on the thenar and hypothenar eminences), Roth spots (exudative, edematous hemorrhagic lesions of the retina with pale centers), or rheumatoid factor Microbiologic evidence: Positive blood cultures not meeting major criterion or serologic evidence of active infection with organism consistent with infective endocarditis [5] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Aortic Valve Endocarditis Surgical Treatment -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Definite IE by clinical criteria Definite IE by clinical criteria is met when 2 major criteria, 1 major criterion plus 3 minor criteria, or 5 minor criteria are fulfilled according to the Modified Duke Criteria. [16] [17] [18] The primary clinical criteria include: Multiple positive blood cultures A single positive culture for Coxiella burnetii Evidence of endocardial involvement, such as new valvular regurgitation or echocardiographic findings of vegetation, abscess, or dehiscence [18] Minor clinical criteria encompass the following: Predisposing heart conditions or intravenous drug use Fever greater than 38 °C (100.4 °F) Vascular phenomena like arterial emboli, septic pulmonary infarcts, or Janeway lesions Immunologic phenomena such as Osler nodes, Roth spots, or rheumatoid factor Positive blood cultures that do not meet the significance criteria [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Rheumatic Fever -- History and Physical -- Cardiac Manifestations
retrieved_docs.content: The transition from ARF to RHD occurs when valvular lesions evolve over 10 to 20 years or during multiple episodes of ARF. RHD is the most common cause of acquired valvular disease, affecting the mitral valve most frequently. Due to severe pancarditis or valvulopathy, cardiomyopathy and heart failure can occur, even during ARF.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Rheumatic Fever -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: When antibodies against GAS surface antigen bind to the surface of heart valves, they upregulate vascular cell adhesion molecule 1 (VCAM1), which allows T cells to adhere to the endocardium and form granulomatous depositions characteristic of ARF, termed Aschoff bodies. [32] These Aschoff bodies contain areas of active inflammation with fibrinoid necrosis, lymphocytic infiltration, plasma cells, and enlarged macrophages with condensed nuclei termed Anitschkow cells. The chromatin within the nucleus of the Anitschkow cells may appear ribbon-like and resemble a caterpillar; therefore, these cells are also called caterpillar cells. [35] Over time, valvular fibrosis, histiocytosis, and neovascularization suggest developing chronic disease and RHD. [36] The classic histopathology of skin biopsies of erythema marginatum shows a polymorphous infiltrate of neutrophils and mononuclear cells in the papillary and upper half of the reticular dermis. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Infectious Endocarditis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Predisposing conditions such as underlying valvular abnormalities, structural heart disease, or intravenous drug use. Fever defined by a temperature greater than 38 degrees celsius. Evidence of vascular phenomena such as mycotic aneurysms, intracranial hemorrhage, Janeway lesions, major arterial emboli, or septic pulmonary infarcts. Evidence of immunologic phenomena such as Osler’s nodes, Roth spots, glomerulonephritis, or positive rheumatoid factor. Positive blood cultures that do not satisfy the aforementioned major criterion or serologic evidence of infection consistent with infectious endocarditis. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Rheumatic Fever -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The most common complicating sequela of ARF is RHD, which occurs 10 to 20 years after the original illness and is due to valvular damage by severe or recurrent bouts of ARF. RHD is the most common cause of acquired valvular pathology worldwide. RHD can lead to heart failure, pulmonary hypertension, dysrhythmias, embolic strokes, and sudden cardiac death. [84]

retrieved_docs.title: Tricuspid Valve Endocarditis -- Evaluation -- Modified Duke Criteria
retrieved_docs.content: Major criteria Positive blood culture (any of the following): Two separate blood cultures yielded positive results for organisms known to cause IE Organisms consistent with endocarditis from blood cultures obtained 12 hours apart A positive culture for organisms that are common skin contaminants in 3 or the majority of ≥4 separate blood cultures (first and last sample drawn at least 1 hour apart) Single positive blood culture of Coxiella burnetti Evidence of endocardial involvement (vegetation, abscess, new valvular regurgitation, dehiscence of a prosthetic valve) Minor criteria Fever >38 °C (100.4 °F) Immunologic occurrence: Roth spots, Osler nodes, glomerulonephritis Predisposing heart defect or IVDU Vascular phenomena: septic emboli, arterial emboli, conjunctival hemorrhages, splinter hemorrhages, Janeway lesions, mycotic aneurysm A positive blood culture that does not meet essential criteria

retrieved_docs.title: Rheumatic Heart Disease -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic heart disease results from either a single or repeated attacks of rheumatic fever that results in rigidity and deformity of valve cusps, the fusion of the commissures, or shortening and fusion of the chordae tendineae. Over 2 to 3 decades, valvular stenosis and/or regurgitation results. In chronic rheumatic heart disease, the mitral valve alone is the most commonly affected valve in an estimated 50% to 60% of cases. Combined lesions of both the aortic and mitral valves occur in 20% of cases. Involvement of the tricuspid valve occurs in about 10% of cases but only in association with mitral or aortic disease. Tricuspid valve cases are thought to be more common when recurrent infections have occurred. The pulmonary valve is rarely affected.

retrieved_docs.title: Rheumatic Heart Disease -- Evaluation -- Definite Rheumatic Heart Disease (either A, B, C, or D)
retrieved_docs.content: A: Pathological mitral regurgitation and at least two morphological features of rheumatic heart disease of the mitral valve B: Mitral stenosis mean gradient greater than or equal to 4 mmHg C: Pathological aortic regurgitation and at least two morphological features of rheumatic heart disease of the aortic valve D: Borderline disease of both the aortic valve and mitral valve

retrieved_docs.title: Interventricular Septum Abscess -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: There are several risk factors for developing infective endocarditis. These include rheumatic, congenital, and degenerative valve lesions, intracardiac prosthetic devices, intravenous drug use, and use of access devices (e.g., indwelling catheters, hemodialysis, pacemakers, etc.). [6] Staphylococcus aureus is the most common cause of infective endocarditis. Data from greater than 70 million hospitalizations in the United States from 1999 to 2008 suggest an increase in the incidence of Staphylococcus aureus rate of infection relative to other microorganisms. In another study on endocarditis, Staphylococci were implicated in 57.5% of all cases, followed by streptococci and enterococci at 33.3%. [7] Bacterial presence leads to colonization and systemic infection as a consequence of intravascular catheters, surgical wounds, prosthetic devices, hemodialysis, etc. [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Mitral Regurgitation -- History and Physical -- Acute Mitral Regurgitation
retrieved_docs.content: In the case of acute bacterial endocarditis, there will be signs and symptoms of sepsis, such as fevers and chills. A history of intravenous drug abuse is highly prevalent, and patients may also have comorbid conditions that predispose them to immunocompromise, such as diabetes mellitus and alcohol use disorder. Due to the embolization of vegetative material, there may be a variety of additional clinical findings depending on the ultimate fate of the emboli. Brain involvement of the emboli will produce focal neurologic deficits; renal involvement will result in hematuria or oliguria; and cutaneous involvement will result in Janeway lesions or widespread petechiae. In contrast to subacute bacterial endocarditis, acute infections typically occur in patients with structurally normal heart valves, and, as such, rheumatic heart disease and prosthetic valves are less common in this population. Furthermore, because the route of bacterial delivery to the mitral valve passes through the right side of the heart, concomitant lesions of the tricuspid and pulmonic valves are not uncommon, and these can often be appreciated by auscultation of murmurs on physical examination. [27] [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Type II Hypersensitivity Reaction -- History and Physical -- Acute Rheumatic Fever and Rheumatic Heart Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with rheumatic heart disease often develop valvulitis as a result of prior acute rheumatic fever, most commonly affecting the mitral valve and, in 20% to 30% of cases, the aortic valve. Clinical findings may include an apical holosystolic murmur of mitral regurgitation, an apical mid-diastolic rumble of mitral stenosis, and a basal early diastolic murmur of aortic regurgitation. Additional manifestations include dyspnea, atrial fibrillation, thromboembolic stroke, and pulmonary hypertension.

retrieved_docs.title: Roth Spots -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Roth spots although classically associated with infective endocarditis are a nonspecific ophthalmologic finding with multiple etiologies. Retinal vessel rupture with whole blood extravasation and fibrin plug development at the site of vessel rupture is the mechanism by which Roth spots occur. A thorough history and physical examination are paramount in diagnosing the underlying disease and guiding treatment in patients with Roth spots. If Roth spots are found on routine ophthalmologic examination referral to a primary care physician for evaluation and work-up of systemic disease is suggested. Identification of the underlying etiology is important in patients found to have Roth spots so that management can be guided to prevent any systemic or ocular complications that might occur. Roth spots are usually asymptomatic and resolve with treatment of the underlying condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Rheumatic Heart Disease -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the aberrant immune response to Streptococcus pyogenes infection in the pathophysiology of rheumatic heart disease. Outline the patient history associated with rhematic heart disease. Summarize the echocardiographic criteria for the diagnosis of rheumatic heart disease. Explain the importance of improving care coordination amongst the interprofessional team to stress the importance of strict follow-up to improve outcomes in those with rheumatic heart disease. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2293
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic carditis, acute rheumatic pancarditis, acute rheumatic valvulitis, الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحاد (acute rheumatic pericarditis), and الْتِهابُ الشَّغافِ الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحادّ (acute rheumatic endocarditis) are all manifestations of acute rheumatic heart disease that share a common clinical course characterized by a sudden onset and/or short duration. This abrupt and brief presentation is a key feature across these related conditions, aiding clinicians in recognizing the rapid evolution of rheumatic involvement of the heart’s structures and informing timely diagnostic and therapeutic decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 253
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic fever (الحُمَّى الروماتيزمية) is a frequent etiologic factor for chronic rheumatic valvulitis, a form of heart valve disorder localized in the heart valves (srdeční chlopně). The disease manifests as rheumatic heart valve stenosis (التَضَيُّق) and rheumatic mitral valve obstruction, both classified as structural disorders of the heart and closely related to each other. Surgical management of these valvular pathologies is organized under heart valve procedures (عمليات صمام القلب), which include broad categories such as Excision of heart valve and Other operations on valves of heart, and more specific sub‑procedures: tricuspid valve replacement (إعاضة الصمام الثلاثي الشرف), pulmonary valve replacement (إعاضة الصمام الرئوي), aortic valve replacement (استبدال الصمام الأبهري), mitral valve replacement (استبدال الصمام التاجي), and closed heart valvotomy of the mitral valve. Both aortic and mitral valve replacements are clinically associated with rheumatic fever, reflecting the need

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2259
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic rheumatic valvulitis and rheumatic heart valve stenosis are rheumatic cardiac conditions that both involve the heart valves (srdeční chlopně). Chronic rheumatic valvulitis is described as being located in the heart valves, indicating that the inflammatory process primarily affects these structures. Rheumatic heart valve stenosis is identified as having its finding site in the heart valves, reflecting that the narrowing or obstruction caused by rheumatic disease occurs at the valve level. Together, these entities highlight the significance of rheumatic involvement of the heart valves in the pathogenesis, diagnosis, and management of valvular heart disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2583
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic rheumatic valvulitis is classified as a type of heart valve disorder and is situated in the srdeční chlopně (the heart valves). Rheumatic heart valve stenosis is also a specific form of heart valve disorder, with its pathological findings located in the srdeční chlopně. Rheumatic mitral valve obstruction is similarly linked to heart valve disorders and is identified as a structural disorder of the heart, reflecting its impact on the mitral valve’s function. Together, these rheumatic conditions illustrate how inflammatory processes can produce structural abnormalities and stenotic lesions within the heart valves, emphasizing their relevance to the broader category of heart valve disorders and structural heart disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2617
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic mitral valve obstruction is classified as a structural disorder of the heart, reflecting damage to the mitral valve caused by rheumatic disease that leads to obstruction of blood flow through the valve. Recognizing this condition as a specific type of cardiac structural abnormality is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate management, and prevention of complications such as heart failure and arrhythmias.

retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic fever (الحُمَّى الروماتيزمية) is a primary etiologic factor for chronic rheumatic valvulitis, a structural disorder of the heart that affects the srdeční chlopně (heart valves) and is classified as a type of heart‑valve disorder. Manifestations of rheumatic heart‑valve disease include rheumatic heart‑valve stenosis and rheumatic mitral valve obstruction, both localized to the mitrální chlopeň (mitral valve) and mapped to the broader category of structural heart disorders. Because of the valve damage produced by rheumatic disease, a spectrum of عمليات صمام القلب (heart valve procedures) is employed. retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic fever (الحُمَّى الروماتيزمية) initiates chronic rheumatic valvulitis, a primary inflammatory process that damages the heart valves (srdeční chlopně) and gives rise to heart valve disorders. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1995
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic rheumatic valvulitis is a long‑standing inflammatory disease that specifically involves the heart valves (srdeční chlopně). As a sequela of rheumatic fever, the condition leads to progressive fibrosis, thickening, and dysfunction of the affected valves, which can result in stenosis or regurgitation and ultimately compromise cardiac performance. Recognizing that chronic rheumatic valvulitis is localized to the srdeční chlopně is essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate imaging of valve morphology, and targeted therapeutic strategies aimed at preserving valve function and preventing heart failure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 194
retrieved_docs.content: The heart (srdce) is presented as the central anatomic site for a wide spectrum of cardiac conditions, diagnostic procedures, and therapeutic interventions. It encompasses the mitral valve (mitrální chlopeň) and other cardiac valves (srdeční chlopně) as well as the pericardium (perikard). Structural cardiac disorders located in the heart include bacterial myocarditis (التهاب عضل القلب الجرثومي), rheumatic disease of the heart, rheumatic mitral valve obstruction, chronic rheumatic valvulitis, rheumatic heart valve stenosis, unspecified mitral valve disease (Aandoening van mitralisklep, niet gespecificeerd), and heart murmurs (نفخة قلبية). Rheumatic heart valve stenosis and chronic rheumatic valvulitis are classified under heart valve disorders, which also contain unspecified mitral valve disease and rheumatic mitral valve obstruction; these disorders are associated with stenosis (التَضَيُّق) and are anatomically situated in the cardiac valves (srdeční chlopně). retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 941
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic rheumatic valvulitis is classified as a heart valve disorder and is located in the heart valves (srdeční chlopně). Rheumatic heart valve stenosis is also a type of heart valve disorder (inverse isa) and its pathological findings are situated in the heart valves (srdeční chlopně). Heart valve disorders commonly affect the mitral valve (mitrální chlopeň). Surgical management of these disorders includes the closed heart valvotomy of mitral valve, a procedure that is performed on the mitral valve (procedure_site_of) and represents a narrower, specific subset of heart valve procedures. Heart valve procedures in general are directed toward the heart valves (srdeční chlopně). Together, these relationships illustrate that chronic rheumatic valvulitis and rheumatic heart valve stenosis are disease entities affecting the heart valves—particularly the mitral valve—and that targeted interventions such as closed mitral valvotomy are integral components of therapeutic care for heart valve disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2610
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic mitral valve obstruction is a structural disorder of the heart that is classified within the broader category of heart valve disorders. This condition reflects rheumatic disease affecting the mitral valve, leading to obstruction of blood flow, and is recognized as a specific type of structural heart disorder, emphasizing its relevance in cardiology and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2513
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic heart valve stenosis and rheumatic mitral valve obstruction are specific rheumatic conditions that affect the cardiac valves. Both entities are classified within the broader category of heart valve disorders, indicating that they represent pathological processes involving valve function. In addition, rheumatic mitral valve obstruction is identified as a structural disorder of the heart, reflecting that it entails anatomical alteration of the mitral valve apparatus. Together, these relationships illustrate that rheumatic valve disease can present as stenosis or obstruction, and that such manifestations are encompassed by the general concepts of heart valve disorders and structural heart disease, which is important for accurate diagnosis, classification, and management in clinical practice.
